ui.language.loadError: The selected language could not be loaded.
learner.localNotice: Progress is saved only in this browser. No registration is required and no data is sent to a server.
learner.dialog.title: What should we call you?
learner.dialog.description: Your name and progress will be stored only in this browser.
exercise.errors.runtimeLoad: Python could not be loaded. Check the internet connection.
exercise.errors.timeout: The code stopped after exceeding the time limit.
exercise.errors.worker: The Python runner failed to start.
lessons.windows-setup.title: Windows 10/11 setup
lessons.windows-setup.intro: Prepare a clean Windows development environment with PowerShell and VS Code.
install.windows-setup.steps.0.title: Confirm the operating system
install.windows-setup.steps.0.body: These instructions are written and tested for 64-bit Windows 10 and Windows 11. Press Win + R, run winver and confirm the version.
install.windows-setup.steps.1.title: Open PowerShell
install.windows-setup.steps.1.body: Open Start, type PowerShell and run the normal application. Administrator mode is not required for the project commands in this trail.
install.windows-setup.steps.2.title: Install VS Code
install.windows-setup.steps.2.body: Download the Windows User Installer. It is the recommended option for most users and does not require administrator permissions. After installation, close and reopen PowerShell.
install.windows-setup.steps.3.title: Create a workspace folder
install.windows-setup.steps.3.body: Keep course projects under one simple path without unusual permissions or cloud synchronization during the first lessons.
install.windows-setup.title: Before installing tools
install.followInOrder: Complete the steps in order. Do not continue when a verification command fails.
meta.description: Professional Python learning path from zero to production
lessons.install-git.title: Install and configure Git
lessons.install-git.intro: Install Git for Windows and configure the identity attached to commits.
install.install-git.steps.0.title: Download Git for Windows
install.install-git.steps.0.body: Use the official Git download page. Choose the 64-bit installer unless the computer specifically uses Windows on ARM.
install.install-git.steps.1.title: Important installer choices
install.install-git.steps.1.body: Keep Git Credential Manager enabled. Allow Git from the command line and third-party software. The remaining defaults are suitable for this course.
install.install-git.steps.2.title: Configure commit identity
install.install-git.steps.2.body: Use your real display name and an email address verified in GitHub. These values become part of future commits.
install.install-git.steps.3.title: Verify Git
install.install-git.steps.3.body: Open a new PowerShell window. A version number confirms that Windows can find Git through PATH.
install.install-git.title: Install once, verify immediately
lessons.install-uv-python.title: Install uv and Python
lessons.install-uv-python.intro: Install uv, use it to install Python, and avoid conflicting global environments.
install.install-uv-python.steps.0.title: Install uv
install.install-uv-python.steps.0.body: Run the official standalone installer in PowerShell. The command temporarily bypasses the script execution policy only for this installation process.
install.install-uv-python.steps.1.title: Restart PowerShell
install.install-uv-python.steps.1.body: Close every open terminal and open a new PowerShell window so the updated PATH is loaded.
install.install-uv-python.steps.2.title: Install Python with uv
install.install-uv-python.steps.2.body: Let uv install the project Python version. Do not download a separate Python installer for this trail.
install.install-uv-python.steps.3.title: Prove Python runs
install.install-uv-python.steps.3.body: uv can execute Python even when the global python command is not configured. This keeps project tooling predictable.
install.install-uv-python.title: One tool owns Python
lessons.github-account-repo.title: GitHub account and first repository
lessons.github-account-repo.intro: Create and verify a GitHub account, then create an empty public repository.
install.github-account-repo.steps.0.body: Open GitHub, select Sign up and complete the account flow. Choose a professional username because it becomes part of repository and Pages URLs.
install.github-account-repo.steps.1.body: Open the verification email from GitHub. An unverified address can block basic actions such as creating a repository.
install.github-account-repo.steps.2.body: Select the plus menu, New repository, name it hagpy-first-site and choose Public. Do not add README, .gitignore or a license because the local project will provide the first commit.
install.github-account-repo.steps.3.body: Keep the Quick setup page open and copy the HTTPS URL. Replace USERNAME in later commands with your GitHub username.
install.github-account-repo.title: Create the remote home first
lessons.github-account-repo.pageTitle: GitHub account and first repository - HagPy
lessons.verify-installation.title: Verify the installation
lessons.verify-installation.intro: Run one checklist that proves Git, uv, Python and VS Code are available.
install.verify-installation.steps.0.body: Close VS Code and every terminal, then open PowerShell again. This prevents an old process from using an outdated PATH.
install.verify-installation.steps.1.body: Every command must print a version and return to the prompt without an error. The exact version numbers may be newer than the examples in the course.
install.verify-installation.steps.2.body: Both values must be present before the first commit. Use the same verified email associated with GitHub.
install.verify-installation.steps.3.body: Continue only after all six values are printed: four tool versions, a name and an email address.
install.verify-installation.title: Do not continue with a broken setup
lessons.setup-troubleshooting.title: Installation troubleshooting
lessons.setup-troubleshooting.intro: Fix the most common PATH, authentication, remote and GitHub Pages failures.
install.setup-troubleshooting.steps.0.bo: Close and reopen PowerShell first. Then ask Windows where the executable is. If no path is printed, reinstall only the missing tool.
install.setup-troubleshooting.steps.1.bo: Configure both values and run the commit again. The email should match a verified GitHub email.
install.setup-troubleshooting.steps.2.bo: Inspect the existing address. Change it instead of adding a second origin.
install.setup-troubleshooting.steps.3.bo: Confirm that the GitHub repository was created empty. If it contains an initial README, do not use force. For a first course project, create a new empty repository and connect its URL.
install.setup-troubleshooting.steps.4.bo: Confirm the repository is Public, index.html is at the selected root, Pages uses main and /(root), and the URL ends with the repository name. Check the Actions tab for a failed pages build.
install.setup-troubleshooting.steps.5.bo: Before asking for help, copy the exact error and these command results. Do not send passwords, tokens or browser authentication codes.
install.setup-troubleshooting.title: Diagnose, do not reinstall blindly
lessons.project-setup.title: Project setup and library management
lessons.project-setup.intro: Create the first local course project with uv and open it in VS Code.
install.project-setup.steps.0.title: Go to the workspace folder
install.project-setup.steps.0.body: Start from the simple workspace path created earlier. Keeping course projects together makes paths, commands and screenshots easier to compare.
install.project-setup.steps.1.title: Initialize the project with uv
install.project-setup.steps.1.body: Create a minimal uv project without Git. The --bare option creates only pyproject.toml, while --vcs none leaves Git initialization for the GitHub lesson.
install.project-setup.steps.2.title: Enter the project folder
install.project-setup.steps.2.body: Commands that create files must run from inside the project folder. Verify that pyproject.toml exists before continuing.
install.project-setup.steps.3.title: Open the project in VS Code
install.project-setup.steps.3.body: Open VS Code from the project root so the editor, terminal and future Git commands all point at the same folder.
install.project-setup.title: Create the first project folder
lessons.project-setup.pageTitle: Project setup and library management - HagPy
lessons.uv.title: Dependencies with uv
lessons.uv.intro: Manage reproducible environments, dependency groups and upgrades.
lessons.uv.blocks.0.title: The core idea
lessons.uv.blocks.0.paragraphs.0: Manage reproducible environments, dependency groups and upgrades.
lessons.uv.blocks.1.title: Working example
lessons.uv.blocks.2.title: Key points
exercise.local.title: Practice in your local project
exercise.local.body: Follow the lesson commands in the project you created with uv. This topic depends on the operating system, network, Git or a running server and is intentionally not simulated in the browser.
lessons.first-html-site.title: Build a local website
lessons.first-html-site.intro: Create index.html in the project and preview it through a local HTTP server.
install.first-html-site.steps.0.title: Create index.html
install.first-html-site.steps.0.body: In VS Code create index.html at the project root. Paste the complete document and save it.
install.first-html-site.steps.1.title: Preview through HTTP
install.first-html-site.steps.1.body: Run a local server from the project directory. Keep the terminal open and browse to the local address. Stop the server later with Ctrl + C.
install.first-html-site.steps.2.title: Verify before Git
install.first-html-site.steps.2.body: The browser must show the Hebrew heading. If it does not, confirm that the file is named exactly index.html and that PowerShell is in the correct directory.
install.first-html-site.title: Build the first local page
lessons.push-first-repository.title: Push the project to GitHub
lessons.push-first-repository.intro: Initialize local Git, create the first commit and push main to the empty repository.
install.push-first-repository.steps.0.bo: Run these commands from inside hagpy-first-site. git status should show index.html as an untracked file.
install.push-first-repository.steps.1.bo: Stage the files, inspect the status and commit one coherent change.
install.push-first-repository.steps.2.bo: Use the HTTPS URL copied from GitHub. Verify origin before pushing.
install.push-first-repository.steps.3.bo: Push the commit. Git Credential Manager may open the browser for GitHub authentication. Complete it and return to PowerShell.
install.push-first-repository.steps.4.bo: Refresh the repository page. It must show index.html and the Create first website commit.
install.push-first-repository.title: From files to history
lessons.push-first-repository.pageTitle: Push the project to GitHub - HagPy
lessons.publish-github-pages.title: Publish with GitHub Pages
lessons.publish-github-pages.intro: Publish the main branch from the repository root and open the live website.
install.publish-github-pages.steps.0.bod: Open the hagpy-first-site repository, select Settings and then Pages under Code and automation.
install.publish-github-pages.steps.1.bod: Under Build and deployment select Deploy from a branch. Choose main and /(root), then Save.
install.publish-github-pages.steps.2.bod: GitHub creates a Pages deployment. The first deployment usually takes a few minutes and can sometimes take longer. Do not repeatedly change the settings.
install.publish-github-pages.steps.3.bod: The project site normally uses this address. Replace USERNAME with the account name. Repository names and paths are case-sensitive.
install.publish-github-pages.steps.4.bod: Edit index.html, commit and push. GitHub Pages will automatically publish the new main commit.
install.publish-github-pages.title: Turn a repository into a website

lessons.variables-types.title: Variables and types
lessons.variables-types.intro: Model values clearly with names and built-in types.
reference.variables-types.objectives.0: Distinguish a value from a variable.
reference.variables-types.objectives.1: Use clear names and recognize common built-in types.
reference.variables-types.terms.0: The data itself, such as 24 or "Maya".
reference.variables-types.terms.1: A meaningful name that refers to a value.
reference.variables-types.terms.2: Putting a value behind a name with =.
reference.variables-types.terms.3: The kind of value and its useful operations.
reference.variables-types.mistakes.0: Using vague names like x when student_name or total_price says what the value means.
reference.variables-types.mistakes.1: Expecting "5" + 2 to work; convert deliberately with str or int.
reference.variables-types.mistakes.2: Using = for comparison. Use == when checking equality.
reference.variables-types.exercise.hints: An f-string can include values: f"{city}".
reference.variables-types.summary.0: Values are data; variables are names for data.
reference.variables-types.summary.1: int, float, str, bool, and None represent different kinds of values.
reference.variables-types.summary.2: A hint such as score: int = 82 documents the intended type.
reference.variables-types.plain: A value is the data itself: 24, "Maya", True, or None. A variable is a name that currently refers to a value. In Python, = is assignment: read it right to left. Python evaluates the value on the right, then makes the name on the left refer to it.
reference.variables-types.basic.lead: Names sit on the left and values on the right. The example uses five common types before inspecting two of them.
reference.variables-types.basic.lines.0: student_name holds text, so its type is str.
reference.variables-types.basic.lines.1: completed_lessons is a whole number: int; progress has a decimal point: float.
reference.variables-types.basic.lines.2: is_active is a Boolean value, and None deliberately means “no value chosen yet”.
reference.variables-types.basic.lines.3: type is an inspection tool: it reports a value’s type, not the value itself.
reference.variables-types.why: Programs handle names, scores, prices, and missing information. A variable gives each useful value a meaningful name, so later code can use it without repeating or guessing.
reference.variables-types.practical.lead: A name can later refer to a different value. Text and numbers are separate types, so converting a number to text must be explicit.
reference.variables-types.practical.line: score starts as 82. Python adds bonus, then score refers to 87.
str(score) converts the number to text so it can join the text message.
reference.variables-types.practical.outp: It prints Score: 87. Writing "82" would create text, not a number.
reference.variables-types.try: Change bonus to -10. Predict the new score before running the program.
reference.variables-types.exercise.promp: Create city, temperature, and has_rain. Print one message containing all three values.

lessons.functions.title: Functions
lessons.functions.intro: Create small units with explicit inputs, outputs and one responsibility.
reference.functions.objectives.1: Tell parameters, arguments, return values, and print apart.
reference.functions.objectives.2: Use defaults, keywords, scope, and type hints.
reference.functions.terms.0: Named code that does one job when called.
reference.functions.terms.1: A named input in a definition.
reference.functions.terms.2: The actual value passed in a call.
reference.functions.terms.3: The result sent back to the caller.
reference.functions.mistakes.0: Using print where another part of the program needs a reusable result.
reference.functions.mistakes.1: Giving one function unrelated jobs; split calculation, validation, and display.
reference.functions.mistakes.2: Using a list or dictionary as a default value; the next lesson explains safe alternatives.
reference.functions.exercise.hints.1: number % 2 == 0 is already a Boolean value.
reference.functions.summary.0: Focused functions take clear inputs and return results.
reference.functions.summary.1: Parameters are in definitions; arguments are in calls.
reference.functions.summary.2: Prefer return for reusable data and keep scope small.
reference.functions.plain: def defines a function but does not run its indented body. A call runs it. A parameter is the name in the definition; an argument is the value supplied in a call. return sends data back to the caller. print only displays text for a person.
reference.functions.basic.lead: This function receives a score and returns a label. Showing the label is deliberately a separate job.
reference.functions.basic.lines.0: score: int and -> str document intended input and output types.
reference.functions.basic.lines.1: score is local to this call; the condition chooses a returned string.
reference.functions.basic.lines.2: 72 is the argument assigned to parameter score. print happens after the result returns.
reference.functions.basic.output: The output is passed. Replacing return with print would leave label as None.
reference.functions.why: Copying a calculation into several places makes later changes risky. A function gives one job a name and one home, then any caller can reuse its result.
reference.functions.practical.lead: A default makes an input optional when one choice is usually right. Keyword arguments make multi-value calls easy to read.
reference.functions.practical.lines.0: greeting has a default, so the first caller may omit it.
reference.functions.practical.lines.1: The second call names both arguments, making their roles explicit.
reference.functions.practical.output: It prints Hello, Maya! and Shalom, Noa!. name exists only while its call runs: local scope.
reference.functions.try: Call welcome with greeting="Welcome". Then try to print name outside the function and read the NameError.
reference.functions.exercise.prompt: Write is_even(number: int) -> bool. Return True for an even number; do not print from the function.

lessons.classes.title: Classes and objects
lessons.classes.intro: Use classes when state and behavior belong together.
reference.classes.objectives.0: Know when a class is useful and when it is not.
reference.classes.objectives.1: Create objects with __init__, self, attributes, and methods.
reference.classes.objectives.2: Protect valid state and meet composition.
reference.classes.terms.0: A blueprint describing objects and behavior.
reference.classes.terms.1: One concrete value created from a class.
reference.classes.terms.2: Data stored on an object, such as self.balance.
reference.classes.terms.3: A function on a class that works with an object.
reference.classes.mistakes.0: Creating a class merely to group unrelated functions; a module is often clearer.
reference.classes.mistakes.1: Forgetting self or asking callers to pass it; Python passes the receiving object.
reference.classes.mistakes.2: Letting outside code freely alter important state; validate near the changing method.
reference.classes.exercise.hints.0: Store the list on the object: self.books = [].
reference.classes.summary.0: A class joins meaningful state and behavior.
reference.classes.summary.1: __init__ establishes state and self identifies one object.
reference.classes.summary.2: Composition lets objects contain or use other objects and is often simpler than inheritance.
reference.classes.plain: A class is a blueprint. An object, also called an instance, is one concrete thing made from it. __init__ runs when Python creates an object and sets its starting attributes. self means this particular object; methods use it to read or change that object’s state.
reference.classes.basic.lead: Every Enrollment has its own learner name and completed count. Calling a method changes only the object receiving the call.
reference.classes.basic.lines.0: class defines a blueprint; no object exists until Enrollment("Maya").
reference.classes.basic.lines.1: __init__ stores initial attributes on self. mark_complete is behavior that changes this object.
reference.classes.basic.lines.2: Python passes maya as self automatically when maya.mark_complete() is called.
reference.classes.basic.output: The output is 1. A second Enrollment starts at 0 because objects own separate state.
reference.classes.why: Use a class when a concept has state that changes over time and rules for changing it: an account, cart, or enrollment. A one-off calculation needs a function, and a record without behavior may be clearer as a dictionary or later a dataclass.
reference.classes.practical.lead: Methods should guard the rules of the state they change. This account rejects an invalid deposit before its balance changes.
reference.classes.practical.lines.0: The default gives a safe starting balance; callers can also supply one.
reference.classes.practical.lines.1: The guard stops invalid input before self.balance changes.
reference.classes.practical.lines.2: Only a valid deposit updates this account.
reference.classes.practical.output: It prints 125. deposit(-25) raises ValueError and preserves the old balance.
reference.classes.try: Create a second Account with 50, deposit 10 only into it, and print both balances.
reference.classes.exercise.prompt: Create ReadingList. __init__ starts self.books as an empty list. add_book(title) adds only a non-empty title and otherwise raises ValueError.

lessons.mocking.title: Mocking and test doubles
lessons.mocking.intro: Replace slow or external boundaries, not your own business logic.
lessons.mocking.blocks.0.title: The core idea
lessons.mocking.blocks.0.paragraphs.0: Replace slow or external boundaries, not your own business logic.
lessons.mocking.blocks.1.title: Working example

lessons.mocking.blocks.2.items.1: Use specs to catch invalid calls
lessons.mocking.blocks.2.items.2: Fakes can be clearer than deep mocks

lessons.coverage.intro: Use coverage to find unexecuted risk, not to manufacture a perfect score.
lessons.coverage.blocks.0.title: The core idea

lessons.fastapi-architecture.blocks.2.it: Framework code stays at the edge

lessons.fastapi-errors.blocks.0.title: The core idea

lessons.production.title: Production readiness
lessons.production.intro: Combine configuration, security, logs, health checks and repeatable deployment.
lessons.production.blocks.0.title: The core idea
lessons.production.blocks.0.paragraphs.0: Combine configuration, security, logs, health checks and repeatable deployment.
lessons.production.blocks.1.title: Working example
lessons.production.blocks.2.title: Key points
lessons.service-layer.title: Service Layer
lessons.service-layer.intro: Move business use cases out of HTTP routes and keep them testable.
product.service-layer.sections.0.title: The problem with a fat route
product.service-layer.sections.0.body: A beginner often validates input, creates IDs, checks duplicates, writes a file and builds the response inside one route. It works once, but the same use case cannot be called from a script, background job or test without pretending to send HTTP.
product.service-layer.sections.1.title: The service owns a use case
product.service-layer.sections.1.body: A service method receives application data, applies business rules and coordinates dependencies. It does not know whether the caller was FastAPI, a CLI or a test.
product.service-layer.sections.2.title: The route becomes an adapter
product.service-layer.sections.2.body: The route converts the Pydantic request into a command, calls the service, and converts the result into a response model. HTTP errors are mapped at this outer boundary.
product.service-layer.mistakes.0: Do not return HTTPException from a service
product.service-layer.mistakes.1: Do not create one service method for every repository method
product.service-layer.mistakes.2: Do not turn the service into a bag of unrelated static functions
product.service-layer.title: Why routes should not run the business
product.service-layer.intro: A route understands HTTP. A service understands what the application is trying to do. Separating them keeps rules reusable and tests fast.
product.service-layer.exercise: Write a CreateDream service that rejects an empty normalized title and saves exactly once. Test it with a fake repository.
lessons.repository-pattern.title: Repository Pattern and skeleton
lessons.repository-pattern.intro: Hide persistence behind a small interface that the service can depend on.
product.repository-pattern.sections.0.ti: Start from what the service needs
product.repository-pattern.sections.0.bo: Do not copy the entire database API into a repository. Define only operations required by real use cases. A small contract is easier to implement and replace.
product.repository-pattern.sections.1.bo: The in-memory repository is not a mock. It behaves like a small real storage implementation and makes service tests readable.
product.repository-pattern.sections.2.bo: Keep domain models, service contracts and infrastructure implementations in separate modules. The dependency arrow points inward: infrastructure implements the application contract.
product.repository-pattern.mistakes.0: Do not expose database sessions to services
product.repository-pattern.mistakes.1: Do not return database rows when the contract promises domain objects
product.repository-pattern.mistakes.2: Do not create a generic repository before real needs exist
product.repository-pattern.title: The application should not know where data lives
product.repository-pattern.intro: A repository presents persistence as a small collection-like interface. The service depends on that contract, not on JSON, MongoDB or PostgreSQL.
product.repository-pattern.exercise: Implement InMemoryDreamRepository and prove save/get round-trip, missing ID, and replacement behavior with pytest.
lessons.secrets-env.title: Secrets, API keys and .env
lessons.secrets-env.intro: Keep credentials outside code and outside Git history.
product.secrets-env.sections.0.title: Why hard-coded keys are dangerous
product.secrets-env.sections.0.body: A Git commit remains in history even after the visible line is deleted. If a real key is committed, treat it as exposed: revoke or rotate it immediately, then remove it from history if necessary.
product.secrets-env.sections.1.title: .env for local development
product.secrets-env.sections.1.body: A local .env file provides values to your process. It is a convenience file, not encryption. Anyone who can read the file can read the secret.
product.secrets-env.sections.2.title: Ignore the real file, document the shape
product.secrets-env.sections.2.body: Commit .env.example with names and safe placeholders so another developer knows what is required. Add .env to .gitignore before the first commit.
product.secrets-env.sections.3.title: Typed settings fail early
product.secrets-env.sections.3.body: pydantic-settings reads environment values and validates them at startup. Missing required configuration should stop the app with a clear error instead of failing during the first user request.
product.secrets-env.mistakes.1: Do not put production secrets in .env inside a container image
product.secrets-env.mistakes.2: Adding .env to .gitignore does not remove a file already committed
product.secrets-env.title: A secret is data the code needs but Git must never remember
product.secrets-env.intro: API keys, passwords and production URLs change between environments and grant access to real resources. They belong in environment configuration, not source code.
product.secrets-env.exercise: Create .env.example and Settings. Run once without a required key and confirm startup fails clearly, then add the local value and verify success.
lessons.frontend-fastapi.title: Frontend and FastAPI
lessons.frontend-fastapi.intro: Understand the complete path from a browser event to a FastAPI response.
product.frontend-fastapi.sections.0.body: When the user submits a form, JavaScript reads its fields, builds a request and waits. FastAPI validates the body, calls the service and returns a response. JavaScript then updates the DOM.
product.frontend-fastapi.sections.1.body: Frontend and backend should agree on URL, method, request JSON, success JSON and error JSON. Swagger helps inspect the contract, but the contract must still be designed.
product.frontend-fastapi.sections.2.titl: One service can serve both sides
product.frontend-fastapi.sections.2.body: For the first full-stack project, FastAPI can serve index.html and static JavaScript as well as /api routes. This gives one origin, one process and one deployment.
product.frontend-fastapi.mistakes.0: Do not put business rules only in JavaScript
product.frontend-fastapi.mistakes.1: Do not let the frontend depend on database details
product.frontend-fastapi.mistakes.2: Do not return a different response shape for every error
product.frontend-fastapi.title: Two programs cooperate through one contract
product.frontend-fastapi.intro: The frontend runs in the browser and manages interaction. FastAPI runs on the server and protects rules and data. HTTP and JSON connect them.
product.frontend-fastapi.exercise: Draw the exact request-response flow for creating one dream, including loading, 201 success, 422 validation and network failure.
lessons.fetch-api.title: The Fetch API
lessons.fetch-api.intro: Send asynchronous browser requests and inspect the Response correctly.
product.fetch-api.sections.0.title: The smallest useful GET
product.fetch-api.sections.0.body: await pauses only the async function, not the browser. The page can continue painting and responding while the network request is in flight.
product.fetch-api.sections.1.title: Check HTTP before trusting the body
product.fetch-api.sections.1.body: fetch rejects on network failures, invalid URLs and similar transport problems. It does not reject merely because the server returned an error status.
product.fetch-api.sections.2.title: A Response body is a stream
product.fetch-api.sections.2.body: Call response.json() once and keep the parsed value. Trying to read the same response body twice causes an error because the stream was already consumed.
product.fetch-api.mistakes.0: Do not solve CORS with mode: no-cors
product.fetch-api.mistakes.1: Do not assume fetch rejects on 404
product.fetch-api.mistakes.2: Do not forget to encode user-controlled URL segments
product.fetch-api.title: fetch sends a request; it does not decide whether the result is good
product.fetch-api.intro: fetch returns a Promise for a Response. A 404 or 500 still produces a Response, so code must inspect response.ok or response.status.
product.fetch-api.exercise: Implement requestJson(url, options) that handles 204, parses JSON once and throws an ApiError containing status and body.
lessons.json-data.title: JSON between frontend and backend
lessons.json-data.intro: Use JSON as a data format without confusing it with JavaScript objects or Python dictionaries.
product.json-data.sections.0.title: The six JSON value kinds
product.json-data.sections.0.body: JSON supports object, array, string, number, boolean and null. It does not directly support UUID, datetime, Decimal, set or Python class instances.
product.json-data.sections.1.title: Serialization and parsing
product.json-data.sections.1.body: FastAPI serializes response models to JSON. In the browser, response.json() parses JSON text into JavaScript values. JSON.stringify performs the opposite direction for a request body.
product.json-data.sections.2.title: Pydantic controls the boundary
product.json-data.sections.2.body: Use Pydantic response models to make output predictable and prevent accidental exposure of internal or secret fields.
product.json-data.mistakes.0: JSON object keys must use double quotes
product.json-data.mistakes.1: Do not manually concatenate JSON strings
product.json-data.mistakes.2: Do not send a Python repr and call it JSON
product.json-data.title: JSON is a text format crossing the network
product.json-data.intro: Python dictionaries and JavaScript objects exist in memory. JSON is their serialized text representation, with a smaller set of supported value types.
product.json-data.exercise: For a DreamRead object, write the exact JSON response and identify which Python values must be converted to strings.
lessons.json-data.pageTitle: JSON between frontend and backend - HagPy
lessons.http-crud-methods.title: GET, POST, PATCH and DELETE
lessons.http-crud-methods.intro: Choose the HTTP method that matches the intent and response.
product.http-crud-methods.sections.0.bod: GET should be safe: refreshing or retrying it must not complete a task, send an email or delete data. Query parameters filter or paginate the result.
product.http-crud-methods.sections.1.bod: Send JSON, declare Content-Type and return 201 with the created representation or its location. Repeating the same POST may create another resource unless idempotency is designed.
product.http-crud-methods.sections.2.bod: PATCH expresses a partial update. Pydantic fields are optional in the update schema, and model_dump(exclude_unset=True) distinguishes missing values from explicit null.
product.http-crud-methods.sections.3.bod: A successful delete can return 204 with no response body. The frontend must not call response.json() for that response.
product.http-crud-methods.mistakes.0: Do not use POST for every action
product.http-crud-methods.mistakes.1: Do not send a request body with GET
product.http-crud-methods.mistakes.2: Do not treat PUT and PATCH as identical
product.http-crud-methods.title: The HTTP method describes the intent
product.http-crud-methods.intro: CRUD is a useful mapping, but HTTP semantics are the real contract. The same URL can support different actions through different methods.
product.http-crud-methods.exercise: Implement createDream, updateDreamStatus and deleteDream using one requestJson helper. State the expected success status for each.
lessons.http-crud-methods.pageTitle: GET, POST, PATCH and DELETE - HagPy
lessons.frontend-loading-errors.title: Loading states and errors
lessons.frontend-loading-errors.intro: Design the interface for waiting, empty results, failures and retry.
product.frontend-loading-errors.sections: Do not infer loading from whether a list happens to be empty. Empty data can be a successful result. Store status and data separately.
Disable the submit button before the request and restore it in finally. This runs after success or failure and prevents the interface from becoming permanently disabled.
Errors must suggest the next action
A network failure may invite Retry. A 422 should point to fields. A 409 explains a conflict. A 500 apologizes and provides a safe retry path without exposing a traceback.
product.frontend-loading-errors.mistakes: Do not replace the whole page with a spinner
Do not show raw exception text to end users
Do not clear user input after a failed submit
product.frontend-loading-errors.title: A network feature has more than success and failure
product.frontend-loading-errors.intro: A usable interface represents idle, loading, success, empty and error states. If one is missing, the user eventually sees a frozen button, stale data or silence.
product.frontend-loading-errors.exercise: Design a state table for loading a dream list and submitting a new dream. Include the visible UI and enabled actions in each state.
lessons.http-status-codes.title: HTTP Status Codes
lessons.http-status-codes.intro: Read status codes as part of the API contract, not decorative numbers.
product.http-status-codes.sections.0.bod: 200 includes a normal response. 201 confirms creation. 204 confirms success with no body. The frontend should handle each deliberately.
product.http-status-codes.sections.1.bod: 400 is a malformed or invalid request not covered by a more specific code. 401 means authentication is required or invalid. 403 means identity is known but not allowed. 404 means the resource is absent. 409 means current state conflicts. 422 is FastAPI validation failure.
product.http-status-codes.sections.2.tit: Server failures are not user mistakes
product.http-status-codes.sections.2.bod: 500 means an unexpected server failure. Log the full exception with a correlation ID, but return a stable safe body. Do not convert every exception into 400.
product.http-status-codes.mistakes.0: 401 is named Unauthorized but usually means unauthenticated
product.http-status-codes.mistakes.1: A 404 should not reveal private resource existence when security forbids it
product.http-status-codes.mistakes.2: Do not return a body with 204
product.http-status-codes.title: A status code summarizes the outcome
product.http-status-codes.intro: The code tells clients which category of result occurred. The JSON body adds details. Returning 200 for every outcome forces every client to invent its own interpretation.
product.http-status-codes.exercise: For create, read missing, duplicate title, invalid body, forbidden update and unexpected database failure, choose a status and explain the contract.
lessons.pydantic-validation-errors.title: Pydantic validation errors
lessons.pydantic-validation-errors.intro: Translate FastAPI 422 details into useful field messages.
product.pydantic-validation-errors.secti: loc usually starts with body, query or path. For a request model field, the remaining location segments identify the field, including nested objects and list indexes.
Create a predictable field key from loc, find the matching form control and place a safe message beside it. Unknown locations go to a form-level summary instead of disappearing.
HTML validation improves speed and usability, but it can be bypassed. Pydantic remains the trusted boundary because requests can come from scripts, other clients or modified browser code.
product.pydantic-validation-errors.mista: Do not show the complete raw 422 response in the page
Do not translate errors by fragile full-message matching
Do not trust frontend validation for security
product.pydantic-validation-errors.title: FastAPI already tells us where validation failed
product.pydantic-validation-errors.intro: A 422 response contains a detail array. Each item identifies the location, error message, error type and sometimes the rejected input. The frontend can turn it into field-level feedback.
product.pydantic-validation-errors.exerc: Given errors for title, steps.0.description and a query parameter, map each to either a field message or form summary.
lessons.same-origin-cors.title: Same Origin and CORS
lessons.same-origin-cors.intro: Understand when the browser blocks a response and configure only trusted origins.
product.same-origin-cors.sections.0.body: Changing http to https, localhost to 127.0.0.1 or port 8000 to 5500 creates a different origin. Paths do not affect origin.
product.same-origin-cors.sections.1.body: For many cross-origin requests, the browser sends OPTIONS with the intended method and headers. If the response does not permit them, the browser stops before exposing the real response to JavaScript.
product.same-origin-cors.sections.2.body: FastAPI CORSMiddleware should list trusted frontend origins. Wildcard is inappropriate for private APIs and cannot be combined with credentialed requests in the normal way.
product.same-origin-cors.sections.3.titl: Prefer same origin for the first deployment
product.same-origin-cors.sections.3.body: Serving frontend and API from the same FastAPI service avoids CORS entirely. Split them later when the architectural benefit justifies the extra configuration.
product.same-origin-cors.mistakes.0: mode: no-cors does not fix readable API responses
product.same-origin-cors.mistakes.1: CORS does not protect non-browser clients
product.same-origin-cors.mistakes.2: Allowing every origin is not authentication
product.same-origin-cors.title: Origin means scheme, host and port together
product.same-origin-cors.intro: Browsers protect pages from reading responses from another origin unless that server explicitly allows it. CORS is a browser permission protocol, not authentication.
product.same-origin-cors.exercise: For frontend localhost:5500 and API localhost:8000, write the exact allowed origin and explain why localhost:3000 is still blocked.
lessons.databases-overview.title: Databases overview
lessons.databases-overview.intro: Choose durable storage according to relationships, queries and operational needs.
product.databases-overview.sections.0.ti: Why a list or JSON file eventually fails
product.databases-overview.sections.0.bo: An in-memory list disappears on restart. A JSON file can survive restart, but concurrent requests can overwrite each other, searching becomes slow, and partial writes can corrupt the file. It is useful as a learning repository, not a production database.
product.databases-overview.sections.1.bo: PostgreSQL is a relational source of truth. MongoDB is a document database with flexible nested records. Redis is primarily an in-memory data structure server often used for cache, short-lived state and coordination. Redis should not automatically replace the durable database.
product.databases-overview.sections.2.bo: Ask what must be unique, which records relate, which updates must succeed together, and which queries dominate. The repository contract lets the application delay the infrastructure choice.
product.databases-overview.mistakes.0: Do not choose NoSQL because it has no schema - the data still has a shape
product.databases-overview.mistakes.1: Do not add Redis before identifying a slow repeated operation
product.databases-overview.mistakes.2: Do not let database-specific types leak through every layer
product.databases-overview.title: Memory ends when the process ends
product.databases-overview.intro: A database stores durable application state and supports safe concurrent access. Choosing one starts from data relationships and queries, not popularity.
product.databases-overview.exercise: For dreams, steps and users, list the relationships, required unique values and two queries. Choose a primary database and defend the choice.
lessons.mongodb-basics.title: MongoDB basics
lessons.mongodb-basics.intro: Learn documents, collections, flexible schemas and when embedding fits.
product.mongodb-basics.sections.0.title: Database, collection and document
product.mongodb-basics.sections.0.body: A database groups collections. A collection groups documents of one application concept. Each document has an _id and may contain nested objects and arrays.
product.mongodb-basics.sections.1.title: Embed or reference
product.mongodb-basics.sections.1.body: Embed steps when they are owned by one dream, loaded together and remain reasonably bounded. Store references when related data grows independently, is shared, or requires separate access patterns.
product.mongodb-basics.sections.2.title: Flexible does not mean inconsistent
product.mongodb-basics.sections.2.body: Pydantic validates API input, while database validation and migrations or update scripts manage stored documents. Old documents may not contain fields introduced later, so reads need intentional defaults.
product.mongodb-basics.sections.3.title: Indexes trade write cost for read speed
product.mongodb-basics.sections.3.body: An index can avoid scanning every document for common filters, but each write must also update indexes. Add indexes for measured query patterns, including uniqueness where required.
product.mongodb-basics.mistakes.0: Do not create one giant document that grows forever
product.mongodb-basics.mistakes.1: Do not assume every document automatically follows the Pydantic model
product.mongodb-basics.mistakes.2: Do not expose ObjectId directly without an API serialization decision
product.mongodb-basics.title: Store related data as documents when it belongs together
product.mongodb-basics.intro: MongoDB stores BSON documents in collections. Documents resemble JSON but support additional types such as ObjectId and dates. Flexible schema means controlled evolution, not absence of validation.
product.mongodb-basics.exercise: Design a MongoDB dream document. Decide whether steps are embedded and define two indexes based on real list and lookup queries.
lessons.postgresql-basics.title: PostgreSQL basics
lessons.postgresql-basics.intro: Learn tables, rows, keys, relationships and transactions.
product.postgresql-basics.sections.0.bod: A dream and its steps have a one-to-many relationship. Keeping steps in their own table allows filtering, ordering and updating one step without rewriting a whole document.
product.postgresql-basics.sections.1.bod: Pydantic protects the API path, but data can also arrive from scripts, migrations or other services. Database constraints remain the final durable guard.
product.postgresql-basics.sections.2.bod: Creating a dream and its initial steps is one business change. If step insertion fails, the dream should not remain half-created. A transaction commits all changes or rolls them back.
product.postgresql-basics.sections.3.bod: Primary keys are indexed, but list pages often need other indexes. An index on owner_id and created_at can serve the common query for one user’s newest dreams.
product.postgresql-basics.mistakes.0: Do not store comma-separated IDs in one text column
product.postgresql-basics.mistakes.1: Do not catch an error and forget to rollback the session
product.postgresql-basics.mistakes.2: Do not add indexes to every column without query evidence
product.postgresql-basics.title: Relationships and guarantees are first-class
product.postgresql-basics.intro: PostgreSQL stores data in typed tables and uses SQL. Primary keys identify rows, foreign keys protect relationships, constraints reject invalid state, and transactions group changes atomically.
product.postgresql-basics.exercise: Design dreams and steps tables with keys, constraints and one useful list index. Describe one operation that must use a transaction.
lessons.redis-cache.title: Redis as a cache layer
lessons.redis-cache.intro: Use temporary fast data to reduce repeated database work without replacing the database.
product.redis-cache.sections.0.title: Cache hit and cache miss
product.redis-cache.sections.0.body: Read Redis first. A hit returns immediately. On a miss, read the database, serialize the result and cache it with a TTL before returning it.
product.redis-cache.sections.1.title: TTL limits staleness
product.redis-cache.sections.1.body: A time-to-live removes an entry automatically. It is a safety net, not a complete invalidation strategy. Shorter TTL reduces staleness but increases database reads.
product.redis-cache.sections.2.title: Invalidate after successful writes
product.redis-cache.sections.2.body: After the database transaction commits, delete affected cache keys. Deleting before a failed database write can cause unnecessary misses; forgetting deletion can return stale data.
product.redis-cache.sections.3.title: Failure should often degrade, not destroy
product.redis-cache.sections.3.body: For ordinary caching, if Redis is unavailable the service can log the failure and read the primary database. Whether to fail open depends on what Redis is used for; locks and rate limits have different safety requirements.
product.redis-cache.mistakes.0: Do not cache secrets or user-specific responses under shared keys
product.redis-cache.mistakes.1: Do not assume Redis persistence makes cache data authoritative
product.redis-cache.mistakes.2: Do not retry Redis forever inside a user request
product.redis-cache.title: A cache is a disposable copy, not the source of truth
product.redis-cache.intro: Redis keeps data in memory and offers fast data structures. In the cache-aside pattern, PostgreSQL or MongoDB remains authoritative and Redis stores a temporary serialized result.
product.redis-cache.exercise: Design keys, TTL and invalidation for GET /dreams/{id} and GET /users/{id}/dreams. Explain behavior when Redis is unavailable.
lessons.redis-cache.pageTitle: Redis as a cache layer - HagPy
lessons.docker-basics.title: Docker basics
lessons.docker-basics.intro: Understand images, containers, Dockerfiles and why reproducible runtime matters.
reference.docker-basics.objectives.0: Explain what Docker packages and what it does not replace.
reference.docker-basics.objectives.1: Distinguish a Dockerfile, an image, and a running container.
reference.docker-basics.objectives.2: Build and run a small FastAPI backend through Docker.
reference.docker-basics.terms.0: A text file containing build instructions for an image.
reference.docker-basics.terms.1: A reusable, immutable package built from a Dockerfile.
reference.docker-basics.terms.2: One running instance created from an image.
reference.docker-basics.terms.3: A connection from a host port to a port inside a container.
reference.docker-basics.mistakes.0: Treating an image as a running server. An image is the package; a container is the running process.
reference.docker-basics.mistakes.1: Copying .env or secrets into an image. Supply secrets through the deployment platform or a secure runtime configuration.
reference.docker-basics.mistakes.2: Binding FastAPI to 127.0.0.1 inside the container. Use 0.0.0.0 so the port mapping can reach it.
reference.docker-basics.exercise.hints.0: Each ignored path belongs on its own line.
reference.docker-basics.summary.0: A Dockerfile builds an image; docker run creates a container from that image.
reference.docker-basics.summary.1: Port mapping exposes a service in a container to your computer or deployment environment.
reference.docker-basics.summary.2: Copy lockfiles before source code, use --frozen, and never build secrets into an image.
reference.docker-basics.visual.alt: Diagram showing an app, libraries, runtime, and configuration packaged into a container that runs the same on a developer machine, server, and cloud.
reference.docker-basics.visual.caption: A container packages the application with the dependencies it needs, then runs the same way in each target environment.
reference.docker-basics.plain: Docker is a tool for running isolated application processes. You write a Dockerfile: a list of instructions for building an image. An image is a read-only package containing files and runtime setup. A container is one running process created from that image. A container is not a virtual machine: it shares the host operating system kernel and starts much faster.
reference.docker-basics.basic.lead: Start with the smallest useful workflow. docker build reads the Dockerfile and creates an image. docker run starts a container from that image. -p 8000:8000 maps port 8000 on your computer to port 8000 inside the container.
reference.docker-basics.basic.lines.0: The final dot means “use the current folder as the build context”. Docker can copy only files from this context.
reference.docker-basics.basic.lines.1: -t gives the new image a memorable name: hagpy-api.
reference.docker-basics.basic.lines.2: --rm removes the stopped container automatically; it does not delete the image.
reference.docker-basics.basic.lines.3: The first 8000 is the host port. The second is the port where the FastAPI process listens inside the container.
reference.docker-basics.basic.output: After the server starts, open http://localhost:8000/docs. FastAPI serves its interactive API documentation from inside the container.
reference.docker-basics.why: Code can work on one computer and fail on another because Python versions, installed packages, operating-system tools, or configuration differ. Docker puts the application and the runtime it needs into a repeatable image. The same image can run on a developer computer, a test server, or a cloud platform.
reference.docker-basics.practical.lead: This Dockerfile is a production-minded starting point for a small FastAPI backend managed by uv. It copies the dependency files first so Docker can reuse that expensive build layer when only application code changes.
python:3.12-slim supplies a small Python runtime. Pin a specific image version in a real deployment policy.
WORKDIR makes /app the current folder for the following commands.
uv is copied into the image, then pyproject.toml and uv.lock are copied before source code.
uv sync --frozen installs exactly what uv.lock describes and fails if the lockfile is out of date.
The application source is copied only after dependencies, so dependency installation can be cached.
0.0.0.0 is essential: it lets Docker forward traffic into the container. 127.0.0.1 would listen only inside it.
reference.docker-basics.practical.output: Build this file from a project that contains pyproject.toml, uv.lock, and src/main.py. The container then runs FastAPI on port 8000.
reference.docker-basics.try: Add a .dockerignore file containing .venv, __pycache__, .git, and .env. Build again and notice that local environments, caches, history, and secrets are no longer sent to Docker.
reference.docker-basics.exercise.prompt: Create a .dockerignore for a Python API. Exclude the virtual environment, Python cache folders, Git history, environment files, and test coverage output.
lessons.docker-fastapi.title: FastAPI in a Docker container
lessons.docker-fastapi.intro: Build and run one portable FastAPI image with uv dependencies.
product.docker-fastapi.sections.0.title: A uv-based Dockerfile
product.docker-fastapi.sections.0.body: Use a small Python base image and copy a pinned uv binary from its official image. Install from uv.lock before source code to preserve the dependency layer cache.
product.docker-fastapi.sections.1.title: Why 0.0.0.0 matters
product.docker-fastapi.sections.1.body: Inside a container, localhost refers to the container itself. Binding only to 127.0.0.1 makes the server unreachable through Docker port publishing. 0.0.0.0 listens on all container interfaces.
product.docker-fastapi.sections.2.title: Configuration enters at runtime
product.docker-fastapi.sections.2.body: The image should be reusable across development and production. Pass environment values when starting the container or through the cloud platform secret manager, never with COPY .env.
product.docker-fastapi.sections.3.title: Stop correctly
product.docker-fastapi.sections.3.body: Container platforms send SIGTERM before stopping a process. Let the server shut down, close connections and stop accepting work. Do not swallow shutdown errors or rely on local files being preserved.
product.docker-fastapi.mistakes.0: Do not run development --reload in production
product.docker-fastapi.mistakes.1: Do not expose a port and forget to bind the application to it
product.docker-fastapi.mistakes.2: Do not copy the whole repository before dependency installation
product.docker-fastapi.title: One FastAPI image should run the same command everywhere
product.docker-fastapi.intro: The container must install locked dependencies, copy only required files, listen on every interface and receive configuration at runtime.
product.docker-fastapi.exercise: Build the image, run it, open /docs, stop it and run it again. Verify that code is present but an unmounted runtime file is not durable.
lessons.docker-fastapi.pageTitle: FastAPI in a Docker container - HagPy
lessons.cicd-overview.title: What is CI/CD?
lessons.cicd-overview.intro: Automate verification and delivery so every change follows the same gate.
product.cicd-overview.sections.0.title: Continuous Integration
product.cicd-overview.sections.0.body: Developers merge small changes frequently. Each push or pull request installs dependencies, checks formatting and linting, runs tests and reports one visible result.
product.cicd-overview.sections.1.title: Delivery versus Deployment
product.cicd-overview.sections.1.body: With Continuous Delivery, a human chooses when to release the verified artifact. With Continuous Deployment, a successful pipeline deploys automatically. The difference is the release decision, not whether tests exist.
product.cicd-overview.sections.2.title: Build once, promote the same artifact
product.cicd-overview.sections.2.body: A Docker image should be built once, identified by an immutable commit SHA and promoted. Rebuilding separately for production can produce different dependencies or bytes than the tested image.
product.cicd-overview.sections.3.title: A pipeline failure is a feature
product.cicd-overview.sections.3.body: A red check prevents an unverified change from reaching users. Do not bypass it to make the dashboard green; fix the code, test or pipeline configuration and preserve the evidence.
product.cicd-overview.mistakes.0: Do not deploy before tests finish
product.cicd-overview.mistakes.1: Do not store cloud credentials in workflow YAML
product.cicd-overview.mistakes.2: Do not allow main to ignore required failing checks
product.cicd-overview.title: Automation turns a checklist into a gate
product.cicd-overview.intro: CI checks every change in a clean environment. Continuous Delivery keeps a verified release ready. Continuous Deployment automatically publishes every change that passes the defined gates.
product.cicd-overview.exercise: Classify these as CI, Delivery or Deployment: PR tests, building an image, manual production approval, automatic deploy from main, and smoke test after deploy.
lessons.github-actions-cicd.title: CI/CD with GitHub Actions
lessons.github-actions-cicd.intro: Create a workflow that installs with uv, checks quality and runs tests.
product.github-actions-cicd.sections.0.b: The name is visible in GitHub. on defines triggers. jobs can run independently or depend on one another. Each job declares a runner and ordered steps.
product.github-actions-cicd.sections.1.t: Install exactly what the project declares
product.github-actions-cicd.sections.1.b: Use setup-uv and the committed uv.lock. --frozen makes CI fail if project metadata and lockfile disagree instead of silently changing dependencies.
product.github-actions-cicd.sections.2.b: Separate steps make the failed responsibility obvious in the GitHub UI. All commands should also work locally so CI is not a mysterious second build system.
product.github-actions-cicd.sections.3.b: Start with a reliable CI workflow. Add Docker build and cloud deployment only after local containerization works. Protect main and require the CI check before merge.
product.github-actions-cicd.mistakes.0: Do not use pull_request_target with untrusted code without understanding its privileges
product.github-actions-cicd.mistakes.1: Do not print secrets or enable shell tracing around them
product.github-actions-cicd.mistakes.2: Do not use continue-on-error for required quality gates
product.github-actions-cicd.title: A workflow is code that describes automation
product.github-actions-cicd.intro: GitHub Actions reads YAML files under .github/workflows. Events start workflow runs, jobs receive fresh runners, and steps execute actions or shell commands.
product.github-actions-cicd.exercise: Create .github/workflows/ci.yml, deliberately break one test, inspect the failed step, fix it and verify the pull request becomes green.
